skill.name: python-packaging
skill.description: Guide Codex, Claude, and Claude Code through modern Python packaging, including pyproject.toml, package layouts, builds, and PyPI publishing workflows.
skill.summary: Build, test, and publish Python packages with modern packaging standards.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many shell, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto indicators, but review found they are markdown documentation examples in SKILL.md rather than executable skill code. The skill is publishable with a medium warning because it teaches package publishing, private indexes, token-based PyPI configuration, and command execution workflows that can affect credentials and external services if followed without care.

medium_findings.title: Publishing and Installation Commands in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The static external command findings are false positives for code execution because the commands are inside markdown examples, not runnable skill scripts. They still create moderate operational risk because users may copy commands that install tools, build distributions, upload packages, remove test environments, or install from package indexes. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines are fenced code blocks and command examples, so there is no direct execution path, but the commands are actionable packaging operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced code blocks and command examples, so there is no direct execution path. The commands are still actionable packaging operations with external side effects.

medium_findings.title: PyPI Token Configuration Template
medium_findings.description: The .pypirc example includes token placeholders and a home-directory configuration path. This is legitimate packaging documentation, but mishandling this file could expose PyPI credentials or encourage storing long-lived tokens locally. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: The evidence is a documentation template rather than credential theft, but it clearly references a sensitive PyPI token configuration file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a documentation template rather than credential theft. It directly references a sensitive PyPI token configuration file.

medium_findings.title: Private and Alternate Package Index Usage
medium_findings.description: The network and non-standard index findings are mostly expected for a Python packaging guide. Using TestPyPI, PyPI, or private indexes is legitimate, but alternate indexes can introduce dependency confusion, credential leakage, or untrusted package installation risk if users do not verify endpoints. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The URLs are shown in package installation and upload examples, not hidden exfiltration logic, but alternate index use is a real supply-chain concern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are shown in package installation and upload examples, not hidden exfiltration logic. Alternate package indexes are still a real supply-chain concern.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts do not identify code that calculates hashes or performs encryption. Reviewed locations are front matter, package metadata, dependency examples, semantic version text, and README badge text, so no cryptographic misuse was found. Confidence: 0.91. Confidence reasoning: Direct line review showed ordinary packaging text, not cryptographic API use or security-sensitive hash logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct line review showed ordinary packaging text, not cryptographic API use or security-sensitive hash logic.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The analyzer labeled many markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution. They are fenced code blocks and inline formatting used to explain Python packaging concepts, not executable instructions embedded in the skill runtime. Confidence: 0.94. Confidence reasoning: The skill has a single SKILL.md file and the cited lines are markdown examples, so the static pattern does not represent direct code execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill has a single SKILL.md file and the cited lines are markdown examples. The static pattern does not represent direct code execution.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Review did not find text that claims system authority, instructs evaluators to ignore prior instructions, or asks the model to skip security review. No prompt injection evidence was found in SKILL.md. Confidence: 0.9. Confidence reasoning: Broad review of the single markdown file found packaging guidance and examples, not evaluator-directed override language.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Broad review of the single markdown file found packaging guidance and examples, not evaluator-directed override language.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Package Publishing Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation includes twine upload and pip install commands that contact external package services. These are expected for this skill, but users should test on TestPyPI, verify repository URLs, and avoid exposing credentials. Confidence: 0.85. Confidence reasoning: The commands are clearly present in documentation examples and have external side effects when copied into a shell.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in documentation examples and have external side effects when copied into a shell.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Credential File Example
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation references creating ~/.pypirc with PyPI token placeholders. This is normal packaging guidance, but token files require careful local permissions and should not be committed. Confidence: 0.87. Confidence reasoning: The sensitive path and token placeholders are explicit, although they are not real credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive path and token placeholders are explicit, although they are not real credentials.

content.value_statement: Python packaging has many standards, tools, and release steps that are easy to miss. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through project layout, metadata, builds, testing, and publishing.
actual_capabilities: Designs source-layout and flat-layout Python package structures.
Drafts pyproject.toml metadata for setuptools, hatchling, flit, and poetry workflows.
Explains CLI entry points using Click or argparse.
Guides local builds for wheels and source distributions.
Documents TestPyPI, PyPI, and private index publishing workflows.
Provides release checklists, README templates, and package data examples.
limitations: It is a documentation-only skill and does not verify packages automatically.
It cannot confirm that PyPI credentials, repository permissions, or CI secrets are configured safely.
It does not replace current Python Packaging Authority documentation for changing standards.
It does not audit package source code for vulnerabilities.

use_cases.title: Package a New Library
use_cases.description: Create a modern package layout, metadata file, test structure, and build workflow for a reusable Python library.

use_cases.title: Release a CLI Tool
use_cases.description: Define console entry points, package command modules, build distributions, and prepare a release checklist.

use_cases.title: Prepare Private Package Publishing
use_cases.description: Document private index configuration, repository URLs, and safer publishing steps for internal packages.

prompt_templates.title: Create Package Skeleton
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-packaging skill to design a source-layout package for my project. Include the folder structure, required metadata, and first build steps.

prompt_templates.title: Write pyproject.toml
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-packaging skill to draft a pyproject.toml for a Python library with runtime dependencies, development extras, classifiers, and package data.

prompt_templates.title: Package a CLI
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-packaging skill to package my CLI tool. Recommend entry points, dependency groups, test commands, and release validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Secure Publishing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the python-packaging skill to review my publishing workflow for TestPyPI, PyPI, and a private index. Focus on tokens, CI secrets, repository URLs, and rollback steps.

output_examples.input: I need to package a small Python library for PyPI.
output_examples.output: A source-layout project plan with required files, package metadata, build commands, test steps, and a release checklist.

output_examples.input: I want my Python tool to install a terminal command.
output_examples.output: A CLI packaging plan with entry point guidance, module placement, dependency extras, and validation steps for installed commands.

output_examples.input: I need to publish to a private package index.
output_examples.output: A private index publishing checklist covering repository URLs, token handling, TestPyPI validation, and dependency confusion precautions.

best_practices: Use a source layout and pyproject.toml for new packages unless a project has a strong reason not to.
Build and test distributions in a clean environment before publishing.
Use scoped API tokens and CI secrets instead of committing package credentials.
anti_patterns: Do not publish directly to production PyPI before testing on TestPyPI or an isolated install.
Do not commit .pypirc, API tokens, or private index credentials to a repository.
Do not pin every runtime dependency exactly unless reproducibility requirements justify it.

faq.question: Does this skill create package files automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and templates that an AI assistant can apply in your project.

faq.question: Can it help with pyproject.toml?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes modern pyproject.toml patterns for metadata, build systems, dependencies, scripts, and tool configuration.

faq.question: Does it support CLI tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Click, argparse, and project script entry points for installed commands.

faq.question: Can it publish packages for me?
faq.answer: It explains publishing commands and checks, but users must control credentials and confirm uploads themselves.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use with private indexes?
faq.answer: Yes, with caution. Verify repository URLs, protect tokens, and avoid mixing trusted and untrusted indexes without review.

faq.question: Does it replace official packaging documentation?
faq.answer: No. It is a practical guide, and users should verify changing standards against current PyPA documentation.
